patient.instructions:

Excess caffeine intake, including afternoon use
Possible subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH) contributing to sleep disturbance and palpitations
Restless legs symptoms risk due to low ferritin
Perimenopausal transition with intermittent night sweats
Suspected obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.4, reports snoring and unrefreshing sleep)

tool_call_sequence.description: Captures the treatment task outcome: prioritize CBT-I and sleep hygiene; if medication is used, it should be time-limited and safety-checked before prescribing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic symptoms when discussing the condition.
Explains first-line treatment as CBT-I and includes specific sleep hygiene guidance (e.g., regular wake time, limit caffeine/alcohol, reduce screen time before bed, stimulus control).

Assesses for common secondary causes and mimics before finalizing treatment (depression/anxiety, substance use, pain, thyroid symptoms, restless legs, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, shift work/circadian issues).
Before prescribing any medication, performs a critical allergy check and does not prescribe if an allergy is present to the proposed agent/excipients.

Before prescribing any medication, performs a critical drug interaction check (including CNS depressants such as alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, sedating antihistamines, antipsychotics) and adjusts/avoids therapy if interactions raise risk.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding, severe hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, untreated sleep apnea, high fall risk/older age, need for driving/operating machinery) and documents rationale if proceeding.
If medication is chosen, frames it as short-term adjunct to CBT-I, uses the lowest effective dose, and avoids inappropriate long-term hypnotic prescribing without reassessment.

Provides clear risk/benefit counseling: next-day sedation, complex sleep behaviors (for Z-drugs), dependence/tolerance risk (for benzodiazepines), anticholinergic burden (for sedating antihistamines), and fall risk in older adults.
Offers nonpharmacologic alternatives and shared decision-making, including preference-sensitive selection (e.g., sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance difficulty).

Creates or recommends a follow-up plan in narrative (even if not using a follow-up tool): reassess response and adverse effects within 2-4 weeks and sooner if severe side effects occur, and outlines escalation if symptoms persist.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, prioritize CBT-I and sleep hygiene; if symptoms are severe or functionally impairing, add a short-term hypnotic after allergy and interaction checks.
must_collect: sleep onset latency / wake after sleep onset / early morning awakening
duration and frequency of symptoms (acute vs chronic)
daytime impairment (fatigue, mood, concentration, safety at work/driving)
current medication list (including OTC sleep aids and supplements)
psychiatric symptoms (anxiety/depression, suicidality screen as appropriate)
OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, daytime sleepiness)
potential drug interactions (CNS depressants, QT-prolonging agents if relevant to choice)
steps: Confirm insomnia features and classify acute vs chronic; assess severity and impairment.
Screen for secondary causes/mimics (OSA, RLS, depression/anxiety, substances, meds, pain, circadian disruption).
Explain diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and set expectations (CBT-I first-line).
Provide CBT-I components and concrete sleep hygiene/stimulus control recommendations.

If medication is indicated: perform allergy_check and drug_interaction_check; consider contraindications; choose a time-limited agent appropriate to sleep-onset vs maintenance problem; counsel on risks and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.
Arrange reassessment within 2-4 weeks; advise earlier follow-up for adverse effects or worsening symptoms; plan to taper/stop medication as CBT-I takes effect.

acceptable_variants.description: CBT-I and sleep hygiene only (no medication) when symptoms are mild/moderate, patient prefers nonpharmacologic care, or risks outweigh benefits.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and assess impairment and duration.

steps: Diagnose insomnia; assess severity and immediate safety concerns (e.g., driving impairment).
Initiate CBT-I/sleep hygiene plan.

Plan reassessment within 1-2 weeks if high risk, otherwise 2-4 weeks; discontinue medication as soon as feasible.
